oob.text:
I'll dig into this for you. Cesspool questions get tangled up between Oregon DEQ rules, City of Portland sewer requirements, and what's actually feasible at your specific lot, so let me check all three angles plus look up your property directly.Now let me check Oregon DEQ's specific rules about cesspool failure, and then look up your specific property on Portland Maps.Now let me look up your specific property in Portland Maps to see what's actually available at 6624 N Missouri Ave.Interesting — the MLS listing from when this property last sold (June 2024) lists the sewer as "Public Sewer." That's a really important detail to flag. But let me double-check the regulatory specifics and connection costs.Let me get the specific connection costs and verify the property's actual sewer situation on Portland Maps.I have enough to give you a solid answer. The short version: yes, almost certainly. But there's a wrinkle at your specific address that's worth checking before you get too deep into planning.

## The regulatory short answer

Within Portland city limits, if your cesspool fails you're required to connect to the public sewer, not replace with a new septic system. Three layers of rule lock this in:

1. **Oregon DEQ (OAR 340-071-0215)**: A failing cesspool is a public health hazard and must be repaired immediately. DEQ will not permit a brand-new cesspool, and a new septic is only allowed where sewer isn't available.
2. **Portland City Code Chapter 17.33**: Whenever a public sewer is available, properties must connect; new septic/cesspool systems aren't an option. Any deferral is automatically terminated the moment a cesspool fails ("in the event of cesspool failure all deferrals will be terminated and connections must occur immediately").
3. **City Code Title 29**: A failing cesspool is treated as a property nuisance, with code enforcement authority to compel abatement.

Public sewer is essentially everywhere in your neighborhood (Piedmont/Humboldt, north of Rosa Parks Way), so the "is sewer available" question won't save you.

## The wrinkle worth checking before you go further

Here's the thing I think is worth flagging: the MLS listing from when 6624 N Missouri last sold in June 2024 reports **Sewer: Public Sewer**. That doesn't necessarily mean it's right (MLS sewer fields are notoriously sloppy), but it raises a real question. Your house was built in 1926, well before public sewer reached much of north Portland, so it almost certainly *started* its life on a cesspool. The question is whether it was already converted to sewer at some prior point and the cesspool merely abandoned in place.

Three ways to settle this in under an hour:

1. **Look at your current water/sewer bill**: If BES is charging you sewer + stormwater (the sewer line is ~$13.54 per 100 cu ft of metered water, plus stormwater fees), you're connected to public sewer. If you're only paying for water, you may still be on a cesspool.
2. **Pull historic plumbing records on portlandmaps.com**: Search the address, scroll to Permits & Zoning > Permits, and check "Historic Plumbing." If there's an old permit titled "Change from cesspool to sewer," the conversion happened. (Sometimes the records are frustratingly handwritten and faint; a $15 in-person trip to PP&D Trade Permits at 1900 SW 4th Ave gets you the full file.)
3. **Get a sewer scope**: $100 to $200, takes about an hour, and gives you a video that definitively shows whether your line goes to a public sewer in the right-of-way or to a buried cesspool. Worth doing regardless; it can also catch root intrusion or cracked clay pipe before they become $20K problems.

A subtle but important catch: even if you're already on city sewer, an old cesspool can still be sitting abandoned on your lot. Portland uses "decommissioned" loosely in historic records; many of these were just stopped up at the building, not actually filled. That's usually fine until you pull a permit (ADU, addition, foundation work) within 10 feet of it, at which point you'll need to formally decommission per OAR 340-071-0185.

## What it actually costs if you do need to connect

Roughly, in 2025-2026 dollars:

- **Private plumbing (contractor work)**: $5,000 to $12,000 typical for the lateral install plus cesspool decommissioning; your lot's small frontage on Missouri keeps this on the lower end.
- **System Development Charge (SDC)**: For a typical residential connection, calculated per drainage fixture unit at ~$343/DFU residential rate (60% of the Tier 1 rate of $572). A standard 3-bath, kitchen, laundry house typically lands in the $4,000 to $7,000 range.
- **Permits**: Septic decommissioning permit, plumbing trade permit, and a UR/UC right-of-way permit. A few hundred dollars total.
- **Possible sewer line / branch charges**: Only if BES has to extend a public main; very unlikely at your address since Missouri is fully sewered.

Realistic total: **$12,000 to $20,000**, on the order of a small kitchen remodel.

## Financial assistance you'd actually qualify for

The City has two loan programs specifically for this scenario, and they're better than most private financing:

- **Private Plumbing Loan**: Up to $20,000 (cap), 6.1% simple interest, 5 or 10-year term, $90 origination fee. Secured as a lien on the property; no income/credit qualification.
- **Safety Net Private Plumbing Loan**: 1.5% interest, deferred payment option for income-qualified or older homeowners. Significantly cheaper if you qualify.
- **City Loan Contract for SDCs**: Up to 20 years to finance the connection charges themselves.

You'd need three bids from CCB-licensed contractors to initiate either loan. The City pays the contractor directly on completion.

## The above-and-beyond move I'd actually recommend

Given your house's age and the ambiguity in the public records, here's what I'd do this month, regardless of whether your system has shown any symptoms:

1. **Spend the $15 at PP&D Trade Permits** (or via GovQA records request if you don't want to drive downtown) to pull every historic plumbing record on the address. You'll learn for sure whether a cesspool was ever permitted, whether/when it was converted, and whether it was actually decommissioned vs. just abandoned.
2. **Get a sewer scope in the next 6 to 12 months** ($100 to $200; recommend asking for a recorded video). Pre-failure scoping costs less than 1% of an emergency dig and gives you negotiating leverage if you ever sell.
3. **If you find an undecommissioned abandoned cesspool**, decide proactively whether to decommission it now (cheaper, on your timeline) or sit on the information (legal, but a future buyer will catch it on their inspection and may discount for the unknown cost).

If you want, I can help you draft the GovQA records request, compile a short list of vetted Portland sewer scope plumbers who specialize in pre-1930 homes, or sketch out the actual cash flow comparison of paying out of pocket vs. taking the BES Private Plumbing Loan. Just say the word.

Two contacts worth keeping handy if it does come to a failure: BES Required Sewer Connection at 503-823-7761 (sewer questions) and Portland Permitting & Development Septic Program at septic@portlandoregon.gov (decommissioning permits).

enhanced.text:
# Cesspool, Sewer, and Your House at 6624 N Missouri Ave

*A friendly walkthrough for a Portland homeowner. Written May 2026.*

---

## Read this first: a surprise from the city's own records

Before answering your question, I checked Portland's permit records and sewer maps for your address. Here's what I found, and it might save you a lot of money:

**The city has a finalized permit on file showing an 80-foot sanitary sewer line was installed at your house in August 2009** (permit number 2009-149664-000-00-PT, "Final Inspection Approved" 8/14/2009). The city's sewer asset map also shows sewer lateral lines (the private pipe that runs from a house to the public sewer) registered to your property. The combined sewer main in the street is about 96 feet from the front of your lot.

What this most likely means: a previous owner (your house was built in 1926) **already converted from cesspool to public sewer in 2009**. What you may be experiencing now is not a failing cesspool serving your home; it could be the **old abandoned cesspool itself collapsing** because it was never properly filled in and decommissioned when the sewer was hooked up. That is extremely common in old Portland houses; people connected to sewer and just left the cesspool sitting in the yard.

This matters because the price tag is wildly different:

- **If you already have sewer and just need to decommission an old cesspool:** roughly the cost of a nice used appliance to a cheap used car, in the $1,500 to $5,000 range, and you'd skip the biggest fees entirely.
- **If you genuinely have an active cesspool and need a brand-new sewer connection:** roughly the cost of a decent used car or more, $15,000 to $30,000+ all in.

So the very first thing to do, before anything else, is **find out which situation you're actually in.** I walk you through how to do that in the "What to do next" list below. Don't pay for plans, bids, or pumping until you know.

The rest of this guide assumes the worst case (you really do have a live cesspool and no working sewer connection), so you have the full picture. But please confirm before spending money.

---

## Plain-English summary

**Short answer: yes, if your cesspool fails and a public sewer line is available within a reasonable distance, Portland requires you to disconnect from the cesspool and hook up to the public sewer.** You can't repair a true cesspool failure and keep using it; cesspools are basically holes in the ground, and once they fail, the city and the state both consider them a public health problem. What that looks like for you as the homeowner: you hire a licensed sewer plumber, the plumber pulls the right city permits, they dig a trench from your house out to the sewer main in the street (probably under N Missouri Ave), connect a new lateral, and properly fill in the old cesspool. You pay for the plumber's labor, a couple of city permits, and a one-time city fee for tapping into the public system. The city has loan programs (including a low-interest one if your income qualifies) that can spread the cost out over 5 to 20 years, so you don't have to pay all of it up front.

Now, the long version, in plain English with code references after each topic.

---

## Is your cesspool actually required to be replaced, or can it be repaired?

The honest answer: a true cesspool can't really be "repaired" in any meaningful way. A cesspool is just a deep hole in the ground (often brick-lined in homes built before about 1950, like yours; sometimes concrete rings) that takes raw sewage straight from your house, lets the solids settle to the bottom, and lets the liquid seep into the surrounding soil. There's no treatment. When one "fails," it usually means either the soil around it is saturated and the liquid won't seep anymore (so it backs up into your house), or the brick or concrete is collapsing.

If a sewer line is reasonably available, the city's general rule is: stop using the cesspool, properly fill it in, and connect to the public sewer instead. Decommissioning a septic or cesspool means pumping out any liquid and solids (using a state-licensed pumper), filling the hole with sand, gravel, or concrete slurry (you can't just use dirt), and getting it inspected. (PCC 17.33.030 is the city rule that sewer connection is required when you've got plumbing fixtures discharging wastewater. OAR 340-071-0185 is the Oregon state rule that requires the cesspool to be decommissioned when it's no longer in use; OAR stands for Oregon Administrative Rules.)

**Who decides if it's "failed"?** The two players are the city and the state, and they have different roles:

- **BES** is the **Bureau of Environmental Services**; this is the city bureau that runs sewers and stormwater. They decide things about the public sewer side: whether sewer is available to your property, what fees you owe to connect, and whether your private connection meets city standards.
- **DEQ** is **Oregon's Department of Environmental Quality**, the state agency that regulates onsite wastewater treatment systems (cesspools, septic tanks, drain fields). DEQ writes the rules for cesspool abandonment.
- **PP&D / BDS** is **Portland Permitting & Development**, formerly called the **Bureau of Development Services**. They handle plumbing permits and the cesspool decommissioning permits and inspections in Multnomah County. (Many Portlanders still call it BDS; you'll see both names. They're the same office.) For Portland addresses, BDS/PP&D is acting as DEQ's local agent on cesspool decommissioning.

In practice: a sewer plumber inspects, your plumbing backs up, or someone notices an obvious problem; you hire a contractor or call the city; BDS/PP&D issues the decommissioning permit and signs off on the fill; BES handles the sewer connection side.

---

## Is there a public sewer line near your property?

**Yes.** Per the city's sewer asset map, there is a combined gravity sewer main running on or near N Missouri Ave, with the closest manhole about 90 feet from your front door and the main line itself about 96 feet away. "Combined" means the same pipe carries both household sewage and stormwater (a 100-year-old neighborhood thing; Portland is gradually separating these but Piedmont still has combined sewers). This main is in the Albina sewer basin.

Practically: you almost certainly have a viable connection path under or across N Missouri Ave. You will not need to extend a sewer main yourself, which is the scenario that gets very expensive.

If the sewer were *not* available, your three options would be: (1) install a fully permitted septic system if soil and lot size allowed (DEQ would have to approve), (2) pay for a public sewer extension to be built to reach your property (very expensive, often $30,000+, sometimes shared with neighbors), or (3) get a private easement to run a line through a neighbor's yard to a sewer they're already connected to. None of those apply here, which is good news. (PCC 17.33.030 covers when sewer connection is required; PCC 17.32 covers what's allowed under what conditions.)

---

## What permits do you need, and from which bureau?

Three bureaus touch this kind of project. Here's what each one does in plain words, and what you (or your contractor) will need from them:

- **BDS / PP&D** (Portland Permitting & Development, the city's building and trade permits office, formerly Bureau of Development Services): issues the **PT permit** (a plumbing trade permit) for the new sewer line *on your private property*, and the **decommissioning permit** for filling in the old cesspool. They also do the inspection. (PCC Title 25, the plumbing regulations.)
- **BES** (Bureau of Environmental Services, the sewer and stormwater bureau): issues the **UC permit** (which stands for "Utility Connection"; this is the sewer connection permit) for the part of the new sewer line that runs in the public right-of-way (the strip of public land between your property and the street, including the sidewalk and the street itself). BES also calculates and collects the **System Development Charge**, often called the SDC. The SDC is a one-time fee the city charges any property tapping into public infrastructure for the first time; it pays for past and future sewer system capacity. (PCC Title 17, public sewer rules; PCC 17.36 specifically covers fees and SDCs.)
- **PBOT** (Portland Bureau of Transportation, the bureau that handles streets and sidewalks): the actual digging in the street is inspected by PBOT right-of-way inspectors. The PBOT side mostly happens automatically as part of the UC permit; you don't apply separately. There is, however, a $295 PBOT inspection fee that started July 1, 2025, baked into the UC permit cost. If your project also needs traffic control or unusual street closure, PBOT may want a separate street use permit. (PCC Title 17 also covers right-of-way work in coordination with PBOT's rules.)

So your sewer plumber will pull a UC permit from BES (with an automatic PBOT inspection role), a PT plumbing permit from BDS, and a decommissioning permit from BDS. A reputable sewer contractor handles all of these; you don't have to chase them yourself.

---

## Which parts of Portland City Code govern this?

A quick plain-English tour, with the title number after each:

- **Sewer rules.** Tells the city how it runs the sewer system, when you have to connect, how connections are permitted, how fees are calculated, and what happens if you don't comply. (PCC Title 17, especially Chapters 17.32, 17.33, and 17.36.)
- **Plumbing rules.** Local plumbing requirements for work on private property; the actual construction standards come from the **Oregon Plumbing Specialty Code (OPSC)** and the **Oregon Residential Specialty Code (ORSC)**, which are the statewide plumbing and residential building codes Portland enforces locally. Your plumber follows these. (PCC Title 25.)
- **Water rules.** Mostly about your domestic water service, not sewer. It comes up here because the new sewer trench can't be too close to your water line, and your plumber has to keep them properly separated. (PCC Title 21.)
- **Tree rules.** Protects street trees and large yard trees. If a street tree near your trenching location is in the public right-of-way, **Urban Forestry** (a group inside Portland Parks & Recreation) has to weigh in before anyone digs near its roots. (PCC Title 11.)
- **BES Sewer, Stormwater & Erosion Control administrative rules.** These are the detailed how-to rules BES writes under Title 17. The most relevant for you are **ENB-4.05** (how SDCs are calculated), **ENB-4.17** (how UC and lateral repair permits work), **ENB-4.27** (nonconforming sewer conversions, which would apply if your existing 2009 connection turned out to have a problem), and **ENB-4.28** (financial assistance programs).
- **State cesspool rules.** Oregon's rules on cesspool abandonment. (OAR 340-071-0185.)

You won't need to read these yourself. Your contractor will. But if a contractor seems unsure what code section applies, that's a sign to keep shopping for a different contractor.

---

## What the work itself looks like

In the worst case (active cesspool, no existing sewer), here is roughly what happens, in order, on the ground:

1. A licensed sewer contractor (look for an active CCB number, the **Construction Contractors Board** license number that all Oregon contractors are required to have) comes out, locates your cesspool, and figures out a route from your house to the public sewer main in the street.
2. They get the necessary permits (UC from BES, PT and decommissioning from BDS).
3. A DEQ-licensed pump truck pumps out the cesspool. You'll get a receipt; save it; the inspector wants to see it.
4. The contractor digs a trench from your house out across your front yard, through the sidewalk, and into N Missouri Ave to reach the sewer main. The trench is typically 4 to 8 feet deep, depending on how deep your sewer main runs. They install a new lateral pipe (the lateral is the private pipe that connects your house to the public sewer main; technically the part inside your property line is yours to maintain, and the part in the street is also yours but the city inspects it).
5. They cut and patch the street and the sidewalk; PBOT inspects this surface restoration work.
6. They fill the old cesspool with approved material (sand, gravel, or concrete slurry; not dirt). They leave the top open for the inspector.
7. BDS comes out for the decommissioning inspection and the plumbing inspection. PBOT comes out for the right-of-way inspection.
8. Once everything passes, the trench gets backfilled and your yard gets put back together (rough, usually; final landscaping is on you).

The whole on-the-ground work, from first digging to inspections done, is typically about 1 to 3 weeks for a straightforward job. Permits and bidding before that can add another 4 to 8 weeks.

---

## Rough cost and timeline

In a worst-case "yes you really do have an active cesspool and no sewer" scenario, here's how the dollars break down for a typical Portland single-family house. **Translation in human terms: about the cost of a decent used car, paid over one summer of weekends if you go fast, or financed by the city over 5 to 20 years if you go slow.**

The biggest line items, roughly:

- **Sewer plumber's labor and materials (the trenching and lateral install, plus the cesspool decommissioning work).** This is usually the single biggest cost. The city itself estimates **$5,000 to $12,000** for the private plumbing work on a normal lot. Your lot is fairly close to the sewer main (about 96 feet to the main, with the manhole at about 90 feet) and not on a slope, so you should land in or near that range rather than above it. **About the cost of one to two months' rent in Portland.**
- **The Sanitary System Development Charge (sanitary SDC) from BES.** This is the one-time tap fee. For a single-family home, BES charges by drainage fixture units (DFUs); a typical Portland house with one or two bathrooms, a kitchen, and a laundry room runs roughly 14 to 22 DFUs. At the **FY 2025-26 Tier 1 residential rate of $572 per DFU**, that's roughly **$8,000 to $12,500**. There may be a stormwater SDC too depending on impervious area, but often modest for an existing house with no new construction. **About the cost of a used Honda Civic.**
- **BES UC permit + PBOT inspection fee.** Currently the UC connection permit plus the new $295 PBOT right-of-way inspection fee adds up to **a few hundred dollars total** when bundled. **About the cost of a nice dinner for a family.**
- **BDS plumbing permit (PT) and decommissioning permit fees.** Usually **a couple hundred dollars combined** for a residential job. **About the cost of one tank of gas, ish, but more.**
- **Pumping the cesspool by a licensed pumper.** Usually **$300 to $600**.
- **Optional: locating an existing cesspool you can't find.** If a contractor can't find your cesspool, hiring a locator service or doing exploratory digging adds **$300 to $1,000**.

**Total ballpark, worst case (real cesspool conversion):** roughly **$15,000 to $30,000**. Most homeowners land in the high teens to low twenties.

**Total ballpark, decommission-only (the scenario your records suggest is more likely):** roughly **$1,500 to $5,000**, mostly the pumper and the fill plus a small permit fee. **About the cost of a good vacation, not a car.**

**Timeline.** From "I want to do this" to "it's done":
- Getting bids and choosing a contractor: 2 to 4 weeks
- Permit application and review: 1 to 4 weeks (UC permits to existing branches review in 1 to 2 business days; longer if connecting to an old clay main)
- Actual construction: 1 to 3 weeks
- Inspections: usually overlap with construction

So **start to finish, roughly 6 to 12 weeks**, or "about one Portland summer." If you want it done before fall rains, start now-ish.

---

## Financial assistance programs (yes, they exist, and they're real)

Portland actually has decent financing for this exact situation. As of this writing, BES offers four programs through Administrative Rule ENB-4.28:

1. **System Development Charge Loans.** For the city tap fee (the SDC). 5, 10, or 20-year repayment, **6.2% interim interest rate** until the city's bond sale, then adjusted. No credit check, no income check, $40 set-up fee plus 0.49% of loan amount, and a small per-bill processing fee. The loan is secured by a lien on your house, but there's no penalty for paying it off early. **This is for almost anybody.**
2. **Private Plumbing Loans.** For the contractor's labor and materials. **Capped at $20,000.** 5 or 10-year terms at a fixed **6.1%** simple interest. Requires three bids from licensed contractors. $90 set-up fee. Same lien situation.
3. **Safety Net Connection Loan.** Income-qualified version of #1, with a **1.5% interest rate**. To qualify in 2025-26 your household income must be at or under: **$86,870 for 1 person, $99,280 for 2, $111,690 for 3, $124,100 for 4** (more for larger households). You also have to live in the house. If you're under 62 you can defer payments for 5 years and re-apply for another 5. If you're 62+ you can defer payments until you sell or transfer the property. **This is genuinely a great program if you qualify.**
4. **Safety Net Private Plumbing Loan.** Same income rules and 1.5% rate, but for the contractor side instead of the city fee.

There's also an interesting wrinkle for your specific property: **6624 N Missouri is inside the Interstate Corridor Urban Renewal Area**, which is a Prosper Portland program area. That doesn't directly fund sewer connections, but it's worth a quick call to ask if any home-repair grants or low-income improvement programs in that area are stackable.

To apply for any of the four BES loans, you start at portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/financial-assistance-sewer-connection-and-conversion. The contact is the **Required Sewer Connection Program** at **503-823-4114**, sewerconx@portlandoregon.gov.

---

## Phasing and sequencing

In the order things actually happen (worst case, full conversion):

1. **Confirm whether you actually have a live cesspool.** Look at your historic plumbing records on PortlandMaps; call BES; potentially have a plumber do a dye test or camera inspection. Do not skip this step.
2. **If sewer connection is needed: get three bids from licensed sewer contractors** (with active CCB numbers and PBOT street use insurance/bond).
3. **Apply for BES financial assistance**, if you want to finance it. The SDC loan and Private Plumbing loan applications can be done before the work starts.
4. **Pay or finance the SDC.** SDCs must be paid (or a financing agreement signed) before BES will issue a UC permit or BDS will issue a plumbing permit.
5. **Your contractor pulls the UC permit from BES** (this has the automatic PBOT inspection assignment).
6. **Your contractor pulls the PT plumbing permit and the decommissioning permit from BDS.**
7. **The cesspool gets pumped** by a DEQ-licensed pumper (keep the receipt).
8. **The trench is dug, the new lateral is installed**, the street is cut and patched, the cesspool is filled.
9. **Inspections happen:** PBOT inspects the right-of-way work; BDS inspects the plumbing and the decommissioning fill.
10. **Inspector signs off**, contractor backfills and restores the yard.
11. **You start receiving sewer/stormwater bills** on your water bill (usually within 3 months).

If you're only doing a decommission-only project (existing sewer already in place), skip steps 2 through 6, and only steps 7, 9, and 10 apply, plus a much smaller BDS decommissioning permit.

---

## Common gotchas Portland homeowners hit

A few things people regularly get burned on:

- **Discovering the sewer main is on the *far* side of the street.** Your trench could be 30 feet longer than expected, which is hours more labor and a much bigger street patch. Have your plumber confirm which side of N Missouri Ave the main is on before bidding.
- **Finding out a neighbor's sewer lateral runs through your yard, or yours runs through theirs.** Old Portland properties sometimes share laterals or have weird routings from the 1920s. PortlandMaps doesn't always show this clearly. A camera inspection helps.
- **Out-of-area plumbers who don't know Portland's quirks.** A plumber from Vancouver or Salem may not know Portland's UC permit process, the cesspool decommissioning rules, or that PBOT (not BDS) inspects right-of-way work. Stick with Portland-experienced sewer contractors. The CCB website lets you look up complaints.
- **Old clay sewer mains.** Some Portland sewer mains in old neighborhoods are clay or even older materials. Connecting to those takes longer to permit (more than the typical 1 to 2 business days), and the contractor may need a deep connection riser or other special fitting. Ask your plumber to check the main's material and depth before bidding.
- **Trees in the right-of-way.** If there's a street tree (planted in the strip between sidewalk and curb) anywhere near where the trench would go, **Urban Forestry** in Portland Parks & Recreation has to weigh in. Cutting roots or digging within the tree's protection zone without their sign-off can cost you a fine on top of construction delays. Note for your house: I can't tell from the data whether you have a street tree, but if you do, factor in 1 to 2 weeks for Urban Forestry review. (PCC Title 11 covers street trees.)
- **Skipping the decommissioning step entirely** (this is the #1 historical sin in Portland). For decades, owners just connected to sewer and abandoned the cesspool in place without filling it. Then 20 years later the cesspool collapses and a sinkhole appears in the front yard, or someone discovers it during a remodel and now it's a code-violation problem. Portland strongly encourages doing the decommissioning at the same time as the connection.
- **Paying for plans before you know what you have.** Get the city's records pulled and at least one camera inspection before paying a plumber to draw plans.

---

## Zoning and overlays at your address

Your property is **zoned R5** (Single-Dwelling 5,000), which is the most common, lowest-restriction residential zone in Portland. Good news: I checked PortlandMaps for everything that could complicate trenching, and you have:

- **No overlay zones** (no environmental conservation, no environmental protection, no design overlay).
- **No flood hazard area.** You are not in a FEMA Special Flood Hazard Area or any city-designated flood storage area.
- **No landslide hazard area.** You are not in the city's potential landslide hazard area or the mapped landslide inventory.
- **No historic district.** Your property is not in a National Register district, conservation district, or local historic district. (Side note: PortlandMaps lists your **neighborhood as Piedmont**, not Arbor Lodge. The border is right around N Rosa Parks Way, just north of you. It's a nitpick but if you ever ask the city about your house, they'll find it under Piedmont.)
- **6% slope, well drained, soil group B.** Easy digging conditions, more or less.
- **Combined sewer basin: Albina.** This means the same pipe takes both sewage and stormwater, which is normal for inner North Portland.
- **Inside the Interstate Corridor Urban Renewal Area** (potential bonus, see Financial Assistance above).
- **Watershed: Columbia Slough.** Not directly relevant to a sewer connection, but it's why BES cares about combined sewer overflows in this area.

Translation: **there is nothing special about your address that makes this project harder than average.** No environmental review, no historic review, no slope hazard. That's about as boring (in a good way) as a Portland cesspool conversion gets. I'd still confirm on PortlandMaps yourself before paying for plans, because data can change.

# What to do next (in the order you should do them)

| # | Step | What it costs | How long | Who | Free? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pull your historic plumbing records on PortlandMaps | $0 | 30 minutes | You | **YES** |
| 2 | Call BES Required Sewer Connection Program to ask for help interpreting the records | $0 | 15 minutes | You | **YES** |
| 3 | If records are inconclusive, get a camera inspection of your sewer line | $200 to $500 | 1 hour | A sewer plumber | No |
| 4 | If you really do need a connection, get 3 bids from licensed sewer contractors | $0 | 2 to 4 weeks | You | **YES** |
| 5 | Apply for BES financing if you want it | $40 to $90 set-up | 2 to 4 weeks | You + BES | Almost free |
| 6 | Pay or finance the SDC | ~$8,000 to $12,500 | At permit time | You + BES | No |
| 7 | Contractor pulls UC, PT, and decommissioning permits | $500 to $800 in fees | 1 to 4 weeks | Contractor | No |
| 8 | Construction and inspections | Built into bid | 1 to 3 weeks | Contractor + city | No |

**Step 1, in detail (do this today, free).** Go to **portlandmaps.com**. Type in 6624 N Missouri Ave. Click on the property tab and look for Permits and Historic Plumbing. Specifically look for:

- The 2009 sewer permit I found (application 2009-149664-000-00-PT, "SANITARY SEWER LINE 80FT")
- Any older "Historic Plumbing" records that mention "DECOMMISSION FILLED" (capital letters, in the narrative or table). If you see "DECOMMISSION FILLED," your old cesspool was already taken care of.
- If you don't see "DECOMMISSION FILLED" anywhere, the cesspool may still be sitting there even though sewer is connected. That's the most likely scenario.

**Step 2, in detail (today or tomorrow, free).** Call **503-823-4114** (BES Required Sewer Connection Program), or email **sewerconx@portlandoregon.gov**. Use the script below. Have your address and Property ID handy: **Property ID R232318**.

**Step 3, in detail.** Only do this if Steps 1 and 2 leave you genuinely confused. Ask the plumber for a "sewer scope" or "sewer camera inspection." They run a camera down your cleanout from inside the house and can tell you whether you're flowing into a sewer line or into a hole in the ground. This is the single best $300 you'll spend.

# Scripts you can read out loud

## Script 1: Calling BES (503-823-4114)

> Hi, I'm a homeowner at **6624 N Missouri Ave, in the 97217 zip code; the property ID is R232318**. I think my house may have a failing cesspool, but I just looked at PortlandMaps and it shows a 2009 sanitary sewer line permit on my property, **so I'm not sure if I'm actually still on a cesspool or if that just means a sewer was installed and the cesspool was never decommissioned**. Can you help me figure out what records you have for my address, whether I'm currently connected to public sewer, and if I am, whether the city has any record of the old cesspool being decommissioned? I also want to understand what I'd need to do if it turns out the cesspool is still there.

## Script 2: Calling BDS / Portland Permitting & Development (503-823-7300)

> Hi, I'm a homeowner at **6624 N Missouri Ave, 97217**. I'm trying to figure out what plumbing permits I'd need for two possible scenarios: **first**, just decommissioning an old cesspool that may already be sitting unused on my property, and **second**, in case I'm wrong about that, doing a full new sewer lateral and cesspool decommissioning together. **Can you walk me through which permits I'd need for each, what the inspection process looks like, and whether the homeowner can apply for a decommissioning permit directly or whether it has to be a contractor?** I'd also like to know what the typical permit fees are.

You can paste either of those into an email if you'd rather not call.

---

# What you might get wrong

A few specific warnings, written as warnings to you, not as code commentary:

1. **Don't assume the records are wrong; assume the records are right and you're confused.** That 2009 sewer permit is not nothing. People in this situation often spend thousands of dollars on a "new" sewer connection they don't need. Verify before you pay anyone for plans.
2. **Don't hire a plumber who doesn't know Portland's permit process.** If they ask you what a UC permit is, hire a different plumber. A sewer-experienced Portland contractor handles all this routinely.
3. **Don't fill the cesspool yourself with dirt to "fix" a sinkhole.** It looks like an easy DIY. It's not. State rules require specific fill materials (sand, gravel, or concrete slurry) and a city inspector has to sign off. Filling it wrong can get you a code violation later and you'll have to dig it back up.
4. **Don't pump the cesspool with anyone who isn't DEQ-licensed.** You need the receipt from a state-licensed pumper for the inspection. An unlicensed pumper saves you maybe $50 and costs you the whole inspection.
5. **Don't skip the three-bid step if you want a city loan.** The Private Plumbing Loan and Safety Net Plumbing Loan both require three bids from licensed contractors. Get three even if you've already decided who you want to use; the city sets the loan limit based on the lowest bid.
6. **Don't forget the SDC has to be paid or financed *before* permits are issued.** People plan their construction timing and forget the SDC step takes a couple of weeks. Start the SDC loan paperwork at the same time you're getting bids.

---

# Friendly accuracy warnings

I tried to be careful, but a few things are worth double-checking yourself before spending money:

- **Sewer availability and the 2009 connection record at your specific address may have nuance I can't see from the public data.** Confirm with BES (503-823-4114) and on PortlandMaps before paying anyone for plans.
- **Cesspool rules are partly state (DEQ) and partly city (BES/BDS).** Make sure your plumber understands the state side too, not just the Portland connection process. Out-of-area plumbers sometimes miss this.
- **The fee numbers I quoted are FY 2025-26.** Portland's fiscal year starts July 1, and rates often change then. The $572-per-DFU SDC rate is current as of this writing but will probably go up modestly in July 2026.
- **The financial assistance program details (income caps, interest rates, terms) are current as of this writing,** but BES updates these. Confirm at portland.gov/bes/connecting-sewer/financial-assistance-sewer-connection-and-conversion.
- **The "year built 1926" and "neighborhood Piedmont" are from the Multnomah County assessor and PortlandMaps,** so they're considered authoritative. The "Arbor Lodge" framing in everyday conversation is fine; the city will look you up under Piedmont.
- **Number of drainage fixture units (DFUs) for your house is a guess.** Your actual SDC depends on the exact count and is calculated by BES based on your plumbing fixtures. If your house has unusual plumbing (extra bathrooms, an ADU, etc.) the number changes.
- **I am not a plumber, a code official, or a lawyer.** I'm a careful researcher. Treat everything here as a starting point, not a final answer.

---

# Glossary

- **BDS / PP&D** – Bureau of Development Services, now known as Portland Permitting & Development. The city office that issues building, plumbing, and decommissioning permits and does inspections on private property.
- **BES** – Bureau of Environmental Services. The city bureau that runs sewers, stormwater, and watersheds.
- **CCB** – Construction Contractors Board. The state board that licenses Oregon construction contractors, including plumbers and sewer contractors. Every legitimate contractor has a CCB number.
- **Cesspool** – A deep hole in the ground (typically 3 to 4 feet wide and 8 to 15 feet deep, brick-lined in older installations) that takes raw sewage from a house. Solids settle, liquids seep into surrounding soil. No treatment. Now considered an environmental and public health problem; new ones aren't allowed.
- **Combined sewer** – A single pipe that carries both sanitary sewage and stormwater runoff. Common in older Portland neighborhoods like Piedmont.
- **DEQ** – Oregon Department of Environmental Quality. The state agency that regulates onsite wastewater treatment, including cesspools.
- **Decommissioning** – Permanently taking a cesspool, septic tank, or seepage pit out of service: pumping it, filling it with approved material, getting it inspected.
- **DFU** – Drainage Fixture Unit. The unit BES uses to count a property's load on the sewer system, based on what plumbing fixtures the house has.
- **Lateral** – The private sewer pipe that runs from your house to the public sewer main. The part inside your property line is yours; the part in the right-of-way is also your responsibility but the city inspects it.
- **OAR** – Oregon Administrative Rules. The state-level rule book.
- **OPSC / ORSC** – Oregon Plumbing Specialty Code and Oregon Residential Specialty Code. The statewide construction codes Portland enforces locally.
- **PBOT** – Portland Bureau of Transportation. Handles streets, sidewalks, and the right-of-way. Inspects sewer connection work in the street.
- **PCC** – Portland City Code. The local rule book.
- **PT permit** – Plumbing Trade permit, issued by BDS for plumbing work on private property.
- **ROW** – Right-of-Way. The public-owned strip of land that includes the street, sidewalk, and the planting strip between them.
- **SDC** – System Development Charge. The one-time fee BES charges to tap a property into the public sewer system. For a Portland single-family home in 2025-26, typically in the $8,000 to $12,500 range.
- **Septic system** – A two-part onsite wastewater system: a septic tank (settles solids) followed by a drain field (lets liquid soak into soil). Different from a cesspool, which has no separate tank or drain field. Septic systems are still legal in some areas; cesspools are not.
- **UC permit** – Utility Connection permit, issued by BES for a new sewer connection in the public right-of-way.
- **UR permit** – Utility Repair permit, issued by BES for repairing an existing lateral in the public right-of-way.
- **Urban Forestry** – A group inside Portland Parks & Recreation that protects street trees and large trees. Has to weigh in on any digging near a street tree.
